nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving shoreline waters of the Colombian Llanos savannah; clear-water streams and tributaries with dense marginal vegetation, leaf litter, and sandy or silty substrates; water is extremely soft and acidic

decorPreferences: spawning caves (coconut shell halves, small clay pots, ceramic tubes)
dense planting with Cryptocoryne, Java moss, Amazon swords
leaf litter (dried Indian almond or oak leaves)
driftwood and roots for territorial division and cover

diet.notes: Primarily carnivorous, feeding on benthic invertebrates in the wild. Offer live and frozen foods as the staple: bloodworms (chironomid larvae), Artemia (brine shrimp), Daphnia, Moina, and mosquito larvae. High-quality micro-pellets and sinking wafers are accepted but live/frozen foods are essential for conditioning and colour. Feed small amounts 2–3 times daily.

goodWith: small peaceful characins (ember tetras, cardinal tetras, pencilfish)
corydoras catfish (bottom zone overlap minimal in large tanks)
dither fish in upper/mid water column
avoidWith: dwarf shrimp and small invertebrates (predation risk)
large or fast-moving fish that cause stress

summary: A rare Colombian Llanos dwarf cichlid from the macmasteri-group, true A. viejita is seldom seen in the trade — most fish sold under this name are actually A. macmasteri. Males are distinguished by a dark dorsal-fin edge and a rectangular caudal-peduncle spot. Intermediate care; best kept in soft, acidic water in a harem with one male and 2–3 females.
careNotes: Apistogramma viejita is a member of the macmasteri-group and originates from the Colombian Llanos — the vast savannah draining into the upper Meta River basin of the Orinoco system. Type-locality water is extremely soft (conductivity below 50 uS/cm) and warm (around 28°C), so the aquarium should replicate these conditions: soft water (gH 1–8, kH 0–4), pH 5.5–6.8, and temperature 24–28°C. Peat filtration, RO water blended with a small amount of minerals, or blackwater extract (Indian almond leaves, driftwood) helps achieve target parameters and brings out breeding behaviour. The minimum footprint for a pair is 60 × 30 cm; for a harem of one male and 2–3 females, 80 × 40 cm or larger is recommended to allow the male to hold a territory while females each claim a cave. Provide multiple small caves (coconut shell halves, unglazed ceramic pots or tubes), dense planting, driftwood, and leaf litter. Gentle filtration — a sponge filter or low-turnover hang-on — is essential; strong currents stress this species. The aquarium should have a tightly fitting lid as these fish can jump when startled. A. viejita is haremic in structure: one dominant male may mate with and loosely defend a group of 2–3 females. Males are significantly larger and more colourful, displaying iridescent blue-green flanks, extended finnage with orange or red margins, and a dark upper dorsal-fin edge. Females are smaller and yellow-gold, intensifying to bright canary yellow with black markings during broodcare. Trade confusion: the vast majority of fish sold as ‘Apistogramma viejita’ in aquarium shops are actually Apistogramma macmasteri (fw-fish-141) or domestic colour strains bred from it (including ‘Red Face,’ ‘Red Mask,’ and ‘Red Shoulder’ variants). True A. viejita is almost never produced commercially and must usually be sourced from specialist hobbyist breeders. Identification: true A. viejita males have a dark top edge to the dorsal fin (absent in A. macmasteri), and the caudal-peduncle spot is rectangular or trapezoid in outline; A. macmasteri’s spot is D-shaped and typically larger. Additionally, true A. viejita displays more red coloration on the head and has shorter caudal-fin extensions compared to A. macmasteri. Buyers should seek fish from known specialist sources and request species verification.
breedingNotes: A. viejita is a substrate cave-spawner. Condition the pair with live and frozen foods for 1–2 weeks prior to pairing. Soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 1–5) and a slight temperature rise to 27–29°C trigger spawning. The female selects a cave and deposits 40–100 eggs on the ceiling or walls; she assumes exclusive broodcare while the male defends the outer territory. Eggs hatch in 36–72 hours depending on temperature; fry become free-swimming after a further 5–7 days. The female leads the fry school closely for 2–3 weeks. First foods: infusoria, micro-worms, or freshly hatched Artemia nauplii. Males should be separated or the tank must be large enough that the female can drive him away from the fry zone. Breeding difficulty is moderate — soft water and conditioning are prerequisites, but the species spawns readily once conditions are met.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Apistogramma viejita
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Rio Yucao tributary, Meta state, Colombia; Puerto Gaitán stock), habitat (shallow slow-moving shoreline waters, Llanos savannah, clear water with conductivity below 50 μS/cm, temperature 28°C), tank minimum (60×30 cm base), diet (Artemia, Daphnia, Moina, bloodworm; dried pellets accepted), breeding (cave spawner, eggs hatch 36–72 h, female exclusive broodcare), compatibility (small characins, rasboras, Otocinclus), and key identification notes distinguishing true A. viejita from A. macmasteri (dark dorsal-fin edge, rectangular/trapezoid caudal spot vs. D-shaped spot in macmasteri; true viejita not commercially produced).

additional.name: FishBase — Apistogramma viejita
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (males listed as 3.0 cm SL, females 4.6 cm SL — note FishBase data appears to have sex labels inverted; males are typically larger in Apistogramma; males can reach ~7 cm TL in the trade), temperature range (23–30°C), pH lower bound (5.5), native range (upper Meta River basin, Orinoco system, Colombia), taxonomy (Cichliformes, Cichlidae, Geophaginae), trophic level 3.3 (carnivore-leaning), fecundity up to 100 eggs, IUCN Least Concern. Used 23–30°C temperature range.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Apistogramma Care Guide
additional.notes: General Apistogramma guide used for trade-standard care parameters: temperature 27–28°C, pH 6.0–7.0, hardness 4–8 dGH, minimum 20-gallon tank for community setup; diet (bloodworms, tubifex, blackworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, sinking pellets); breeding (cave spawner, sponge filter, fry foods: infusoria, vinegar eels, micro-worms, then baby brine shrimp); compatibility (tetras, pencilfish, hatchetfish; avoid bettas and dwarf shrimp). No viejita-specific page; data applied as genus-level baseline.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Apistogramma viejita
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Cichlidae, described Kullander 1979), distribution (Meta River and Orinoco system, Colombia), haremic social structure (one male with multiple females), fecundity (up to 100 eggs deposited on cave ceiling), and trade confusion: A. macmasteri is frequently mislabelled as A. viejita in the aquarium trade; true A. viejita is rarely bred commercially, and most 'viejita color forms' sold are domestic A. macmasteri strains.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Apistogramma viejita
additional.notes: Supplementary hobbyist source. Provided water parameters (temperature 25–28°C, pH 5.5–6.5, hardness 2–10°dH), adult size (6–8 cm per trade measurements for males), minimum tank 60 L, diet (brine shrimp, bloodworms, daphnia, mosquito larvae, blanched vegetables; 2–3 feedings/day), breeding (female guards ~40 eggs; hatch 3–5 days; free-swimming after 5–7 days), harem structure (1 male, 3–4 females), lifespan 2–3 years (up to 4 years optimal). Adult size range 3.5–7.0 cm adopted to span wild females (small) through trade males (larger).

fish.breedingNotes: Cave spawner; female deposits 40–100 eggs on cave ceiling and provides exclusive broodcare. Soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5) and live/frozen food conditioning are prerequisites. Eggs hatch in 36–72 hours; fry free-swimming within a week. First foods: infusoria or micro-worms, then Artemia nauplii.
